did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) based on the DenseNet architecture (DenseNet201 and DenseNet169), to detect and classify skin cancer from dermoscopy images using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation and training of modified DenseNet201 and DenseNet169 models on the HAM10000 and oral histopathology datasets. It includes specific sections for Implementation, Results, and Discussion with reported accuracy metrics (e.g., 93.39% for skin cancer, 94.41% for oral cancer), indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Cancer Detection Using Deep Learning
journal: International Journal for Research in Applied Science & Engineering Technology (IJRASET)
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning system to detect and classify skin cancer from dermoscopy images and oral cancer from histopathological images using modified DenseNet architectures. The researchers implemented pre-trained DenseNet201 and DenseNet169 models, adding custom layers including flatten, dropout, and dense layers with ReLU activation. The models were trained on the HAM10000 dataset for skin cancer and a Kaggle oral histopathology dataset, with data split into 70% training, 20% validation, and 10% testing sets. Evaluation results showed that the modified DenseNet201 achieved the highest accuracy of 93.39% for skin cancer detection at 70 epochs. For oral cancer detection, the modified DenseNet201 achieved an accuracy of 94.41% at 40 epochs, outperforming the DenseNet169 model. The authors concluded that the proposed modified DenseNet201 approach effectively improves cancer detection accuracy for both disease types.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study detects skin cancer using the HAM10000 dataset. The paper mentions melanoma in the introduction regarding survival rates but classifies the HAM10000 images broadly as 'Skin Cancer' vs 'Not Cancer' (or 'Non-cancerous') in the experiments. The HAM10000 dataset inherently contains multiple lesion types (e.g., melanoma, nevus, BCC, etc.), but the paper's experimental labels are binary: 'Skin Cancer' and 'Not Cancer'.

The study detects oral cancer using a Kaggle oral histopathology dataset. The confusion matrix labels explicitly use 'oral_scc' for Oral Cancer and 'oral_normal' for Not Cancer. The dataset consists of 10,002 histopathological images, with 5,001 non-cancerous.
Used as the negative class for both skin cancer detection ('Not Cancer', 'Non-cancerous Dermoscopy Images') and oral cancer detection ('oral_normal', 'non-cancerous' histopathological images).

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic labels: Skin Cancer vs. Not Cancer (for dermoscopy images) and Oral Cancer (oral_scc) vs. Not Cancer (oral_normal) (for histopathological images).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'detect and classify' cancer. The methodology uses a Softmax activation function to normalize output for classification. Results are reported as accuracy percentages based on confusion matrices with discrete class labels ('0'/'1' for skin cancer, 'oral_scc'/'oral_normal' for oral cancer). No continuous regression targets were predicted.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopy image dataset gathered by the Department of Dermatology at the Medical University of Vienna in Vienna, Austria, available via Harvard Dataverse and Kaggle.

use.training: Used for training the modified DenseNet201 and DenseNet169 models for skin cancer detection.
use.validation: Used for validation (20% of the dataset).
use.testing: Used for testing (10% of the dataset).

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Oral Histopathology Dataset
datasets_used.description: A CC0: Public Domain licensed dataset contributed by Ashenafi Fasil Kebede, consisting of histopathological images of the oral cavity.

use.training: Used for training the modified DenseNet201 and DenseNet169 models for oral cancer detection.
use.validation: Used for validation (20% of the dataset).
use.testing: Used for testing (10% of the dataset).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Results were evaluated using accuracy metrics and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.dataset_description: Dermoscopy images for skin cancer detection (10,015 images total). Split: 70% training, 20% validation, 10% testing.
HAM10000.notes: DenseNet201 performed more accurately than DenseNet169. Confusion matrix labels: '0' represents Skin Cancer, '1' represents Not Cancer.

Kaggle Oral Histopathology Dataset.dataset_description: Histopathological images for oral cancer detection (10,002 images total, 5,001 non-cancerous). Split: 70% training, 20% validation, 10% testing.
Kaggle Oral Histopathology Dataset.notes: DenseNet201 performed more accurately than DenseNet169. Confusion matrix labels: 'oral_scc' represents Oral Cancer, 'oral_normal' represents Not Cancer.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models imported from the Tensorflow library
architecture_used.input_size: 128 x 128 pixels for dermoscopy images; 224 x 224 pixels for histopathological images

architecture_details.modifications: Added flatten layer, dropout layer, two dense layers (each with 128 units), batch normalization, and dropout at the end of the pre-trained models
architecture_details.activation_functions: ReLU activation in dense layers; Softmax activation for final output normalization
architecture_details.dense_block_difference: In DenseNet201, dense block 3 iterates 48 times; in DenseNet169, it iterates 32 times

training_details.data_preprocessing: Resizing to 128x128 or 224x224 pixels; normalization by scaling pixel values to 0-1 range (formula: 1/255)

training_schedule.skin_cancer: Trained for 80 epochs; best performance at epoch 70
training_schedule.oral_cancer: Trained for 60 epochs; best performance at epoch 40

classification_or_output_head.purpose: To normalize the output of the network for binary classification (Skin Cancer vs. Not Cancer; Oral Cancer vs. Not Cancer)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing dermoscopy images to 128 x 128 pixels
Resizing histopathological images to 224 x 224 pixels
Normalization by scaling pixel values to the range of 0 to 1 using the formula 1/255
conclusions: The modified DenseNet201 architecture outperformed the modified DenseNet169 architecture in detecting both skin cancer and oral cancer.
The proposed deep learning model achieved an accuracy of 93.39% for skin cancer detection using the HAM10000 dataset.
The proposed deep learning model achieved an accuracy of 94.41% for oral cancer detection using the Kaggle oral histopathology dataset.
The study concludes that the proposed modified DenseNet201 approach effectively improves cancer detection accuracy for both skin and oral cancers.
Future work must address bias by developing datasets that incorporate images of diversified skin tones and environments.
Further testing is required to determine if the proposed modified DenseNet model is effective for diagnosing other types of cancer.
Future research should focus on developing models with continuous learning mechanisms to adapt to new data over time.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a significant population bias limitation, noting that future work must incorporate images of diversified skin tones and environments to remove bias in cancer detection.
The paper lacks external validation; models were trained and tested on the HAM10000 and a specific Kaggle oral histopathology dataset without testing on independent, multi-center clinical cohorts.

The study relies exclusively on Accuracy as the evaluation metric, omitting sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, or AUC, which are critical for assessing diagnostic performance in medical contexts.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the observed performance differences between the modified DenseNet201 and DenseNet169 models.

The authors note that existing research often uses smaller datasets (<5000 images) leading to overfitting, yet this study uses larger datasets (10,015 and 10,002 images) without addressing potential data leakage or class imbalance issues within those datasets.

The clinical relevance is limited by the binary classification approach (Cancer vs. Not Cancer) rather than multi-class lesion typing, which restricts the model's utility for specific diagnostic sub-typing in clinical practice.
The study was conducted using Google Colab, which may introduce variability in hardware environments compared to standardized clinical deployment settings.
